title: Separable differential equations
description: Solve differential equations using separation of variables.
topic_path: Finding general solutions using separation of variables

hints:
We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-\dfrac{e^x}{8}
\\\\
-8\,dy&=e^x\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
-8\,dy&=e^x\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int -8\,dy&=\displaystyle\int e^x\,dx
\\\\
-8y&=e^x+C_1
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{e^x+C_1}{-8}
\\\\
y&=-\dfrac{e^x}{8}+C
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=-\dfrac{e^x}{8}+C$

$\begin{align}
6\,dy&=\dfrac{1}{x-7}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int 6\,dy&=\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x-7}\,dx
\\\\
6y&=\ln|x-7|+C_1
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{\ln|x-7|+C_1}{6}
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{\ln|x-7|}{6}+C
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\dfrac{\ln|x-7|}{6}+C$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{x^2}{9}+\dfrac{7}{9}
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{1}{9}(x^2+7)
\\\\
9\,dy&=(x^2+7)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
9\,dy&=(x^2+7)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int 9\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (x^2+7)\,dx
\\\\
9y&=\dfrac{x^3}{3}+7x+C_1
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{x^3}{27}+\dfrac{7x}{9}+C
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\dfrac{x^3}{27}+\dfrac{7x}{9}+C$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-\dfrac{1}{3}x^3
\\\\
-3\,dy&=x^3\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
-3\,dy&=x^3\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int -3\,dy&=\displaystyle\int x^3\,dx
\\\\
-3y&=\dfrac{x^4}{4}+C_1
\\\\
y&=-\dfrac{x^4}{12}+C
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=-\dfrac{x^4}{12}+C$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-\dfrac{\sin(x+5)}{y}
\\\\
-y\,dy&=\sin(x+5)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
-y\,dy&=\sin(x+5)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int -y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int \sin(x+5)\,dx
\\\\
-\dfrac{y^2}{2}&=-\cos(x+5)+C_1
\\\\
y^2&=2\cos(x+5)+C
\\\\
y&=\pm\sqrt{2\cos(x+5)+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

Also notice that the root extends over the entire expression, including $C$:

$\pm\sqrt{2\cos(x+5)+C} \neq \pm\sqrt{2\cos(x+5)}+C$

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\pm\sqrt{2\cos(x+5)+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-\dfrac{1}{5xy}
\\\\
-5y\,dy&=\dfrac{1}{x}\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
-5y\,dy&=\dfrac{1}{x}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int -5y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx
\\\\
-\dfrac{5y^2}{2}&=\ln|x|+C_1
\\\\
y^2&=-\dfrac{2\ln|x|}{5}+C
\\\\
y&=\pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{2\ln|x|}{5}+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

Also notice that the root extends over the entire expression, including $C$:

$\pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{2\ln|x|}{5}+C} \neq \pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{2\ln|x|}{5}}+C$

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{2\ln|x|}{5}+C}$

$\begin{align}
y\,dy&=x^{-3}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int x^{-3}\,dx
\\\\
\dfrac{y^2}{2}&=-\dfrac{1}{2x^2}+C_1
\\\\
y^2&=-\dfrac{1}{x^2}+C
\\\\
y&=\pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{x^2}+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

Also notice that the root extends over the entire expression, including $C$:

$\pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{x^2}+C} \neq \pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{x^2}}+C$

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\pm \sqrt{-\dfrac{1}{x^2}+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{x^2}{10y}-\dfrac{2x}{5y}
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{1}{10y}(x^2-4x)
\\\\
10y\,dy&=(x^2-4x)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
10y\,dy&=(x^2-4x)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int 10y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (x^2-4x)\,dx
\\\\
5y^2&=\dfrac{x^3}{3}-2x^2+C_1
\\\\
y^2&=\dfrac{x^3}{15}-\dfrac{2x^2}{5}+C
\\\\
y&=\pm \sqrt{\dfrac{x^3}{15}-\dfrac{2x^2}{5}+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

Also notice that the root extends over the entire expression, including $C$:

$\pm \sqrt{\dfrac{x^3}{15}-\dfrac{2x^2}{5}+C} \neq \pm \sqrt{\dfrac{x^3}{15}-\dfrac{2x^2}{5}}+C$

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\pm \sqrt{\dfrac{x^3}{15}-\dfrac{2x^2}{5}+C}$

$\begin{align}
-4y^2\,dy&=e^{x}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int -4y^2\,dy&=\displaystyle\int e^{x}\,dx
\\\\
-\dfrac{4y^3}{3}&=e^x+C_1
\\\\
y^3&=-\dfrac{3e^x}{4}+C
\\\\
y&=\sqrt[3]{-\dfrac{3e^x}{4}+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\sqrt[3]{-\dfrac{3e^x}{4}+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{3}{xy^2}
\\\\
y^2\,dy&=\dfrac{3}{x}\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
y^2\,dy&=\dfrac{3}{x}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int y^2\,dy&=\displaystyle\int \dfrac{3}{x}\,dx
\\\\
\dfrac{y^3}{3}&=3\ln|x|+C_1
\\\\
y^3&=9\ln|x|+C
\\\\
y&=\sqrt[3]{9\ln|x|+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\sqrt[3]{9\ln|x|+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-\dfrac{x+6}{7y^2}
\\\\
-7y^2\,dy&=(x+6)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
-7y^2\,dy&=(x+6)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int -7y^2\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (x+6)\,dx
\\\\
-\dfrac{7y^3}{3}&=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+6x+C_1
\\\\
y^3&=-\dfrac{3x^2}{14}-\dfrac{18x}{7}+C
\\\\
y&=\sqrt[3]{-\dfrac{3x^2}{14}-\dfrac{18x}{7}+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\sqrt[3]{-\dfrac{3x^2}{14}-\dfrac{18x}{7}+C}$

$\begin{align}
y^2\,dy&=2x\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int y^2\,dy&=\displaystyle\int 2x\,dx
\\\\
\dfrac{y^3}{3}&=x^2+C_1
\\\\
y^3&=3x^2+C
\\\\
y&=\sqrt[3]{3x^2+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\sqrt[3]{3x^2+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-\dfrac{e^x y^2}{10}
\\\\
-\dfrac{10}{y^2}\,dy&=e^{x}\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
-\dfrac{10}{y^2}\,dy&=e^{x}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int -\dfrac{10}{y^2}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int e^{x}\,dx
\\\\
\dfrac{10}{y}&=e^x+C
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}&=\dfrac{e^x+C}{10}
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{10}{e^x+C}
\end{align}$

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\dfrac{10}{e^x+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=5y^2\cos(x)
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y^2}\,dy&=5\cos(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{y^2}\,dy&=5\cos(x)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{y^2}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int 5\cos(x)\,dx
\\\\
-\dfrac{1}{y}&=5\sin(x)+C_1
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}&=-5\sin(x)+C
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{1}{-5\sin(x)+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\dfrac{1}{-5\sin(x)+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{(xy)^2}{8}+y^2
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{y^2}{8}(x^2+8)
\\\\
\dfrac{8}{y^2}\,dy&=(x^2+8)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{8}{y^2}\,dy&=(x^2+8)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \dfrac{8}{y^2}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (x^2+8)\,dx
\\\\
-\dfrac{8}{y}&=\dfrac{x^3}{3}+8x+C_1
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}&=-\dfrac{x^3}{24}-x+C_2
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}&=-\dfrac{x^3+24x+C}{24}
\\\\
y&=-\dfrac{24}{x^3+24x+C}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=-\dfrac{24}{x^3+24x+C}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-\dfrac{2y^2}{x^2}
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y^2}\,dy&=-\dfrac{2}{x^2}\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{y^2}\,dy&=-\dfrac{2}{x^2}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{y^2}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int -\dfrac{2}{x^2}\,dx
\\\\
-\dfrac{1}{y}&=\dfrac{2}{x}+C_1
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}&=\dfrac{-2}{x}+C
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}&=\dfrac{-2+Cx}{x}
\\\\
y&=\dfrac{x}{-2+Cx}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\dfrac{x}{-2+Cx}$

$\begin{align}
e^y\,dy&=2x\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int e^y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int 2x\,dx
\\\\
e^y&=x^2+C
\\\\
\ln(e^y)&=\ln(x^2+C)
\\\\
y&=\ln(x^2+C)
\end{align}$

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\ln(x^2+C)$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{\sin(x)}{e^y}
\\\\
e^y\,dy&=\sin(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
e^y\,dy&=\sin(x)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int e^y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int \sin(x)\,dx
\\\\
e^y&=-\cos(x)+C
\\\\
\ln(e^y)&=\ln(-\cos(x)+C)
\\\\
y&=\ln(-\cos(x)+C)
\end{align}$

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\ln(-\cos(x)+C)$

$\begin{align}
e^y\,dy&=(x+10)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int e^y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (x+10)\,dx
\\\\
e^y&=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+10x+C
\\\\
\ln(e^y)&=\ln\left(\dfrac{x^2}{2}+10x+C\right)
\\\\
y&=\ln\left(\dfrac{x^2}{2}+10x+C\right)
\end{align}$

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\ln\left(\dfrac{x^2}{2}+10x+C\right)$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{x^2}{6e^y}
\\\\
6e^y\,dy&=x^2\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
6e^y\,dy&=x^2\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int 6e^y\,dy&=\displaystyle\int x^2\,dx
\\\\
6e^y&=\dfrac{x^3}{3}+C_1
\\\\
e^y&=\dfrac{x^3}{18}+C
\\\\
\ln(e^y)&=\ln\left(\dfrac{x^3}{18}+C\right)
\\\\
y&=\ln\left(\dfrac{x^3}{18}+C\right)
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\ln\left(\dfrac{x^3}{18}+C\right)$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=4ye^x
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=4e^{x}\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=4e^{x}\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int 4e^{x}\,dx
\\\\
\ln|y|&=4e^x+C_1
\\\\
e^{\ln|y|}&=e^{4e^x+C_1}
\\\\
|y|&=e^{4e^x}e^{C_1}
\\\\
y&=Ce^{4e^x}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=Ce^{4e^x}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=8x^3y-8xy
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=y(8x^3-8x)
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=(8x^3-8x)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=(8x^3-8x)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (8x^3-8x)\,dx
\\\\
\ln|y|&=2x^4-4x^2+C_1
\\\\
e^{\ln|y|}&=e^{2x^4-4x^2+C_1}
\\\\
|y|&=e^{2x^4-4x^2}e^{C_1}
\\\\
y&=Ce^{2x^4-4x^2}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=Ce^{2x^4-4x^2}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=-10x^4y
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=-10x^4\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=-10x^4\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int -10x^4\,dx
\\\\
\ln|y|&=-2x^5+C_1
\\\\
e^{\ln|y|}&=e^{-2x^5+C_1}
\\\\
|y|&=e^{-2x^5}e^{C_1}
\\\\
y&=Ce^{-2x^5}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=Ce^{-2x^5}$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=4x^3y-6x^2y
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=y(4x^3-6x^2)
\\\\
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=(4x^3-6x^2)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=(4x^3-6x^2)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{y}\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (4x^3-6x^2)\,dx
\\\\
\ln|y|&=x^4-2x^3+C_1
\\\\
e^{\ln|y|}&=e^{x^4-2x^3+C_1}
\\\\
|y|&=e^{x^4-2x^3}e^{C_1}
\\\\
y&=Ce^{x^4-2x^3}
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{\cos(x)}{\sin(y)}
\\\\
\sin(y)\,dy&=\cos(x)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\sin(y)\,dy&=\cos(x)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \sin(y)\,dy&=\displaystyle\int \cos(x)\,dx
\\\\
-\cos(y)&=\sin(x)+C_1
\\\\
\cos(y)&=-\sin(x)+C
\\\\
\arccos(\cos(y))&=\arccos(-\sin(x)+C)
\\\\
y&=\arccos(-\sin(x)+C)
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\arccos(-\sin(x)+C)$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{x}{\sin(y)}-\dfrac{9}{\sin(y)}
\\\\
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{1}{\sin(y)}(x-9)
\\\\
\sin(y)\,dy&=(x-9)\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\sin(y)\,dy&=(x-9)\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \sin(y)\,dy&=\displaystyle\int (x-9)\,dx
\\\\
-\cos(y)&=\dfrac{x^2}{2}-9x+C_1
\\\\
\cos(y)&=-\dfrac{x^2}{2}+9x+C
\\\\
\arccos(\cos(y))&=\arccos\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{2}+9x+C\right)
\\\\
y&=\arccos\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{2}+9x+C\right)
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\arccos\left(-\dfrac{x^2}{2}+9x+C\right)$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{x}{7\cos(y)}
\\\\
7\cos(y)\,dy&=x\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
7\cos(y)\,dy&=x\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int 7\cos(y)\,dy&=\displaystyle\int x\,dx
\\\\
7\sin(y)&=\dfrac{x^2}{2}+C_1
\\\\
\sin(y)&=\dfrac{x^2}{14}+C
\\\\
\arcsin(\sin(y))&=\arcsin\left(\dfrac{x^2}{14}+C\right)
\\\\
y&=\arcsin\left(\dfrac{x^2}{14}+C\right)
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\arcsin\left(\dfrac{x^2}{14}+C\right)$

We can bring this equation to the form $f(y)\,dy=g(x)\,dx$:

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{dy}{dx}&=\dfrac{4x^3}{\cos(y)}
\\\\
\cos(y)\,dy&=4x^3\,dx
\end{align}$

This means we can solve this equation using separation of variables!

$\begin{align}
\cos(y)\,dy&=4x^3\,dx
\\\\
\displaystyle\int \cos(y)\,dy&=\displaystyle\int 4x^3\,dx
\\\\
\sin(y)&=x^4+C
\\\\
\arcsin(\sin(y))&=\arcsin(x^4+C)
\\\\
y&=\arcsin(x^4+C)
\end{align}$

Notice that after the integration, more work was required in order to isolate $y$.

In conclusion, this is the solution of the equation:

$y=\arcsin(x^4+C)$
